item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cool and leaves me wanting more. +1

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Very bizarre, completely unexplainable. Also includes the Portuguese language, which is always something I like.
+1

item.title: Aldwych
item.content:encoded: I like the article, but the redaction of the station name is rather pointless. Since you mention that the station was decommissioned in 1994, this narrows the list down to 3 stations. Of these, only one has 7 letters in the name; Aldwych.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Embodys the SCP universe perfectly. Weird and unexplained. You can have my upvote now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: normal can of corn, somebody messed up XD +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The final frontier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One might be Ceres, one might be Charon or maybe Eris, or even some moons that got dislodged from one of the gas giants.

Bet you didn't know there was already a pluto-like dwarf planet between Mars and Jupiter, now you do!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In that case, where are extra planets coming from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so weird, I love it. The inconsistencies with the two halves, the fact that it was BUILT, and not on accident, really cement it for me.

I have a theory that its not actually a doorway to another solar system, but a doorway to Ganymede in our own solar system millions of years in the future, after some catastrophe either alters Jupiter or sends Ganymede into Neptune's orbit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And therein lies another mystery: who said anything about replicating? Someone made these by hand, and there are plenty of indecipherable languages in the real world. It's not a stretch to imagine considering this place is simply not even on Earth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One Nit-pick I have is the mirror universe signage with undecipherable language. Surely if it is replicating the signage there is only a fairly small number of possibilities the signage could be. Even including spelling mistakes I would imagine an approximation based on their location alone would be quite possible. Previously undiscovered language - yes, undecipherable not sold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might want to rein it in a little there. :) I feel like you could've expressed the same thoughts without the accusatory tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But if you're just going to dismiss actual science as "preconceptions", there's no point in trying to argue with that sort of mindset.

Uh… okay? That seemed a bit unnecessarily rude.

And no, I didn't miss the "beyond a trace" bit at all. I'm saying that the Foundation, an organization that deals with things that violate the laws of physics on a daily basis, would not simply stop at "okay, no oxygen, assume no life" because they've seen much, much stranger things before. It's a safe assumption to make if we assume that science is rigid and unyielding, but the nature of the Foundation and its objects and entities in containment gives way to a whole new view of the universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you miss the bit where I said "beyond a microscopic trace"? An oxygen atmosphere requires an ongoing source of oxygen, which means plants. If you want to hypothesize another source of molecular oxygen, you could equally well imagine an inorganic source of any other molecule. After all, amino acids are more common than molecular oxygen in interstellar space.

Or maybe you don't understand the scales involved here - any amount of oxygen in an interstellar cloud would still be a microscopic amount by planetary standards.

But if you're just going to dismiss actual science as "preconceptions", there's no point in trying to argue with that sort of mindset.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You mention testing for "organic components", but if there's any oxygen beyond a microscopic trace, that would be proof of the existence of plant life.

NASA would beg to differ.

While I realize that Earth is the only planet humanity has extensively researched that has significant amounts of breathable molecular oxygen, I somehow doubt that the Foundation would have the same preconceptions. I think they would be looking for amino acids or other chemicals that are far more solid proof of living organisms at work than just oxygen.

I would welcome input from a xenobiologist, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's the composition of the atmosphere on the other side? You mention testing for "organic components", but if there's any oxygen beyond a microscopic trace, that would be proof of the existence of plant life. (For a lifeless world at approximately Earthlike temperature, the most likely components would be carbon dioxide, nitrogen, argon, and methane, probably in that order.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am intrigued and would like to know more, and that is my one auto-upvote rule.

The way the details are almost-but-not-quite consistent is what sold me. Just when I think I have a working theory about what's going on, something throws a spanner in the mental works and it all falls apart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is kind of similar to some other articles, and the faceless bandmembers is unnecessary, but I like the mood on this article. Reminds me of Dark City.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't particularly bothered by the objects recovered not really shedding any light on the situation. However, the thing about the faceless bandmembers was too cliche/unnecessary for me, and somehow managed to completely sour my opinion of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, except I neutral voted. Just didn't seem to have a cohesive story, but the details were enough to make me not… actually, in writing this comment, I'm finding it more and more difficult to rationalize neutral voting so… I've downvoted. Please try to tighten this up, because it could be neat if it had an idea of what it wanted to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because I finally realised why the tube workers kept going on strike

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A copy of the Evening Standard, entirely in reverse but otherwise identical to those printed on December 27, 1993.

All text is otherwise identical to actual posters with the exception of the band members, who are entirely devoid of facial features.

What about photos in the Evening Standard? Do they depict faceless people?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking SCP-1691, mainly because of the reversing, but it's definitely very different apart from that detail.

I have to ask: does this have a backstory? I have absolutely no idea what it could be, which is a very good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a "doorway to weird place" SCP, it works and stands out. I thought the language was a tad verbose and convoluted… except that I had no problem understanding what was going on.

Also, if 1375-133 was creamed corn instead of kernel, it would be way funnier, just sayin'. And then you'd have that "hey, more weird stuff/oh, it's funny/whoa what just happened" whiplash happening. I will agree that the other objects could be a little more noteworthy: one to establish a reference, the 19:30 note, a couple to show that maybe something's going on here, and then -133 to make the reader go "Whoa, wait, hold the phone!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kept waiting for something about this to stand out and be really interesting, but nothing happened. Needs a more solid/explicit story. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this concept and the article itself.

However, I'm actually fairly bored by the addenda. Only the canned corn and "19:30, don't forget" left a mark in my brain.

But I'm not sure if that's an issue, because I enthusiastically upvoted the article based on the main body and being bored by the addenda didn't make me want to remove that upvote. And it's not like the addenda being boring is any kind of suspension of disbelief or logic problem… quite the opposite, in fact. I think I'd love a version of this article that did more with those details even more, but this version is great as it is.

EDIT: Actually, while I thoroughly agree with Smapti that this stands on its own and is not too similar to 1165, structurally 1165 is actually a good comparison to make the point, because 1165's Survey Log is equivalent to this article's addenda. but 1165's Survey Log is thoroughly fascinating in a way that these addenda are not. However I would certainly have upvoted 1165 even if I found its Survey Log boring, and while I would be fascinated to see where else this article could go, one could make the argument that working over the addenda could backfire and perhaps feel unnecessary… So take that FWIW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's weird enough to justify its existence, I think. Not everything has to be instantly deadly and murderous, although obviously we wouldn't want that door unsecured and opened for too long given the pressure difference, but that's part of the containment procedures anyway.

The reverse text is a nice touch, to be sure. Any plans on expanding it? Just curious where you might be going with it, if anywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As the author of 1165, I think it's different enough to stand on its own. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a tad too similar to SCP-1165, but I still liked it enough to upvote.
